microservice resilience.description: Designing systems that gracefully handle failure using transient error policies, circuit breakers, and load throttling.
microservice resilience.tip: Always combine retries with circuit breakers to prevent flooding a failing downstream service.

retry policies.description: Automatically repeats a failed operation, typically with an exponential delay (backoff) and randomization (jitter) to prevent hammering the server.
retry policies.tip: Use jitter to ensure all failing requests don't retry at the exact same millisecond.
retry policies.code: policy => policy.WaitAndRetryAsync(3, retryAttempt => TimeSpan.FromSeconds(Math.Pow(2, retryAttempt)) + TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(new Random().Next(0, 100)))

circuit breakers.description: State machine that blocks requests to a repeatedly failing downstream service, preventing resource exhaustion and allowing the service to recover.
circuit breakers.tip: Configure failure thresholds (e.g. 50% failures in 10s) to trip the breaker dynamically.
circuit breakers.code:
policy => policy.AdvancedCircuitBreakerAsync(
    failureThreshold: 0.5,
    samplingDuration: TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10),
    minimumThroughput: 8,
    durationOfBreak: TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30)
)

bulkhead isolation.description: Limits the number of concurrent executions or resources allocated to a specific service, separating and protecting other modules from failure.
bulkhead isolation.tip: Prevents a single failing service from hogging all Kestrel/ThreadPool execution threads.

fallbacks.description: Provides a degraded alternative action or default value when a call fails, preserving core usability.
fallbacks.tip: Fallback data can be fetched from a local Redis cache or memory snapshot.

domain-driven design.description: Software design approach focusing on translating complex business domain requirements directly into coding model boundaries.
domain-driven design.tip: Structure your C# solution folders to mirror your aggregates and bounded contexts.

aggregates.description: A grouping of associated objects treated as a single transactional unit, managed by an Aggregate Root.
aggregates.tip: Only reference other aggregates by their Identity (IDs), never by direct entity references.

entities vs value objects.description: Entities have a distinct identity and lifecycle, while Value Objects are defined solely by their attributes and are immutable.
entities vs value objects.tip: Use value objects to capture self-contained domain rules (e.g. Email address or Money format validations).

domain events.description: Signals representing a significant business occurrence in the domain, allowing decoupled systems to react.
domain events.tip: Use domain events to coordinate state transitions across different aggregates asynchronously.

strategic boundaries.description: Splitting the organization and software domains into bounded contexts, defining context maps and ubiquitous languages.
strategic boundaries.tip: Use Bounded Contexts to isolate subdomains (like Billing, Shipping) so different teams don't conflict.

distributed locking.description: Synchronizing operations across multiple running container instances to prevent double-processing and race conditions.
distributed locking.tip: Distributed locks require an auto-expiry time (TTL) to prevent permanent deadlocks if the acquiring node crashes.

lock types.description: Optimistic concurrency handles collision checks at save time; pessimistic locks block rows up front; distributed locks coordinate across nodes.
lock types.tip: Use optimistic concurrency (EF RowVersion) as the default; use distributed locks (Redis) for critical operations (like payments).

redlock algorithm.description: A consensus lock algorithm for Redis, securing a resource by acquiring locks on multiple master nodes with a short TTL lease.
redlock algorithm.tip: Acquires locks concurrently across multiple Redis masters; requires success on a majority of nodes.

db-level locks.description: Database engine mechanisms like SELECT FOR UPDATE or transaction table locks to block concurrent writes.
db-level locks.tip: Use database locks inside tight transactions; avoid keeping locks open during external HTTP calls.

deadlock mitigation.description: Using explicit timeouts, ordering locks uniformly, and keeping locks short to prevent execution deadlocks.
deadlock mitigation.tip: Always define a timeout when acquiring a lock; log lock timeout exceptions for diagnostics.

clean architecture.description: Architectural structure separating business logic from frameworks, UI, and database layers using inward-facing dependencies.
clean architecture.tip: Your Domain layer must contain zero NuGet dependencies on Entity Framework or third-party web components.

core layers.description: Domain (core rules/entities) and Application (handlers/use cases) forming the core, framework-free business engine.
core layers.tip: Application layer defines interfaces (e.g. IRepository) which are implemented in external layers.

external layers.description: Infrastructure (database access, API clients) and Presentation (endpoints, controllers) implementing concrete details.
external layers.tip: Use Dependency Injection in Program.cs to bind Infrastructure concretions to Core interfaces.

key principles.description: Flow of dependencies only points inward towards the core. Classes use abstractions rather than concretions.
key principles.tip: Enforce this boundary in your CI pipeline by checking project references.

key benefits.description: High testability via mock interfaces, and independence from database or framework changes.
key benefits.tip: Allows migrating from SQL Server to Postgres by rewriting the Infrastructure project, leaving Core code untouched.

caching strategy.description: Storing computed results or database rows in fast temporary memory to reduce latency and load.
caching strategy.tip: Never cache highly volatile transactional data without strict invalidation rules.

cache types.description: In-Memory cache is inside the app process (fastest); Distributed cache (Redis) is shared across scaled nodes.
cache types.tip: In-memory cache is ideal for static metadata (e.g., countries list); Distributed cache is required for user sessions.

eviction policies.description: Rules to remove items: Absolute Expiration (fixed lifespan) and Sliding Expiration (lifespan extended on access).
eviction policies.tip: Combine sliding expiration with an absolute cap to prevent stale keys from residing in cache forever.

cache patterns.description: Cache-Aside reads from cache first, then database on miss; Write-Through updates cache and database synchronously.
cache patterns.tip: Use Cache-Aside for most general read-heavy endpoints; write-through is best for keeping write/read data closely aligned.

cache stampede.description: When multiple requests miss cache at once, overload database. Solved using locking, GetOrAdd, or pre-warming.
cache stampede.tip: Wrap database calls in SemaphoreSlim or ConcurrentDictionary with Lazy to coordinate thread requests.

solid principles.description: Five guidelines to design clean software: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov, Interface Segregation, and Dependency Inversion.
solid principles.tip: Dependency Inversion: Depend on interfaces, not implementations.

single responsibility (srp).description: A class should have only one reason to change, meaning it should focus on a single job.
single responsibility (srp).tip: If a class requires multiple logger, repository, and helper parameters, it is likely violating SRP.

open/closed (ocp).description: Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification, utilizing interfaces or inheritance.
open/closed (ocp).tip: Use strategy pattern or abstract classes to add new payment processors without altering original controller classes.

liskov substitution (lsp).description: Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without breaking application behavior.
liskov substitution (lsp).tip: Avoid throwing NotImplementedException in subclasses; this is a clear sign of LSP violation.

interface segregation (isp).description: Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces and methods they do not use; prefer thin interfaces.
interface segregation (isp).tip: Break down large interfaces (e.g. IRepository) into thinner, functional interfaces (e.g. IReadRepository, IWriteRepository).

dependency inversion (dip).description: Depend on abstractions rather than concrete classes, decoupling modules using dependency injection.
dependency inversion (dip).tip: Depend on IEmailService in your controller, rather than instantiating a SendGridEmailService directly.

dependency injection.description: Design pattern where a class receives its dependencies from an external container instead of instantiating them itself.
dependency injection.tip: ASP.NET Core has a built-in DI container supporting Constructor and Action/Parameter injection.

lifetimes.description: Transient (new instance each call), Scoped (once per request), and Singleton (single instance for app life).
lifetimes.tip: Injecting Scoped services into Singletons creates a captive dependency bug; avoid this entirely.

benefits.description: Enables loose coupling, cleaner unit testing, and structured object lifespans.
benefits.tip: Mocking external services during unit testing becomes trivial since you inject mocks via interfaces.

usage.description: Injecting dependencies via constructors (most common), method parameters, or HTTP request context attributes.
usage.tip: Use [FromServices] on action parameters to inject services only used in a specific endpoint call.

ef core basics.description: Entity Framework Core is a lightweight, extensible ORM that maps C# objects to relational databases.
ef core basics.tip: Configure entity relationships in OnModelCreating using Fluent API instead of attributes to keep entity classes clean.

core concepts.description: DbContext manages database connections and change tracking; DbSet maps to specific database tables; Entities map to rows.
core concepts.tip: A DbContext is not thread-safe and must be disposed/scoped per request.

database flow.description: Applying migrations to control table schemas, and calling SaveChanges to write pending mutations to the database.
database flow.tip: Always generate migrations via CLI: `dotnet ef migrations add MyMigration`.

querying data.description: Retrieving rows using LINQ expressions, loading tracked instances, or disabling tracking for optimizations.
querying data.tip: Use AsNoTracking() when loading read-only collections to improve query execution speed.

mvc vs web api.description: Comparing server-side web layouts (MVC) and RESTful API endpoints (Web API) in ASP.NET Core.
mvc vs web api.tip: Use Web API for SPAs (React, Angular) or mobile backends; use MVC for server-side rendered websites.

mvc (model-view-controller).description: Renders HTML pages on the server using Model state, Controller actions, and Razor View files.
mvc (model-view-controller).tip: Razor View engine compiles .cshtml files into HTML on the server dynamically.

web api.description: Exposes JSON or XML data endpoints using ControllerBase classes and ApiController attributes for modern APIs.
web api.tip: Always return appropriate IActionResult status codes (e.g. Ok, Created, BadRequest) to clients.

commonalities.description: Both share the ASP.NET Core middleware request pipeline, dependency injection container, and routing systems.
commonalities.tip: You can host both MVC pages and Web API controllers inside the same ASP.NET Core application.

saga pattern.description: Coordinates multiple local transactions across services to achieve eventual consistency. Rollbacks are managed via compensating actions.
saga pattern.tip: Avoid distributed lock contention; prefer asynchronous message-driven architectures.

choreography.description: Decentralized model. Services listen to events from other services and react accordingly without a central coordinator.
choreography.tip: Best for simple, low-step workflows. Watch out for cyclic dependencies and hard-to-trace flows.

loose coupling.description: Services only know about the events they consume, not about the overall business process.

high event latency.description: Difficult to predict total processing time under heavy queues.

orchestration.description: Centralized model. A state machine orchestrator directs the participants on what local transactions to execute.
orchestration.tip: Best for complex workflows. Provides a single source of truth for the saga status.

centralized state.description: State is tracked in one place (e.g. SqlServer, Redis), simplifying tracing.

saga orchestrator.description: Acts as a traffic cop, invoking commands and handling errors.

outbox pattern.description: Guarantees 'At-Least-Once' delivery. Messages are stored in the same database transaction as the state mutation and published by a background processor.
outbox pattern.tip: Crucial for preventing database and message broker consistency mismatch.

atomicity.description: Writing state and event payload to the database succeeds or fails together.

background publisher.description: BackgroundService polls the Outbox table using SKIP LOCKED to publish messages.

compensating transactions.description: Undoes a previously completed local transaction when subsequent steps fail. Must be idempotent.
compensating transactions.tip: Compensating transactions are 'semantic rollbacks' (e.g. issuing a credit refund, not reversing raw DB rows).

backward recovery.description: Reverts actions in reverse order of their execution.

clr memory & gc.description: Automates memory allocation and reclamation. Divided into generations to optimize execution speeds based on object lifespans.
clr memory & gc.tip: Minimize Gen 2 collects and Large Object Heap (LOH) allocations to reduce Stop-the-World pauses.

heap segments.description: Managed memory divided into Ephemeral Heaps (Gen 0, 1), Gen 2, Large Object Heap (LOH), and Pinned Object Heap (POH).
heap segments.tip: POH prevents GC from moving pinned arrays, reducing heap fragmentation.

loh (>= 85k bytes).description: For large objects. Not compacted by default to save CPU cycles, leading to fragmentation.

poh (pinned heap).description: Introduced in .NET 5. Restricts GC compaction issues for interop/buffers.

gc types.description: Workstation GC optimizes for UI responsiveness (single thread). Server GC creates dedicated heaps/threads per logical core to optimize throughput.
gc types.tip: Enable Server GC in production microservices to leverage parallel CPU cores.

workstation gc.description: Runs on a single background thread. Ideal for desktop apps.

server gc.description: One GC thread per CPU core. Highly parallel, memory intensive.

diagnostics.description: Profiling and analyzing memory to fix production leaks, memory bloat, and CPU spikes due to high '% Time in GC'.
diagnostics.tip: Use dotnet-counters for live tracking, and dotnet-dump for offline post-mortem heap analysis.
diagnostics.code:
# Capture a dump on Linux container
dotnet-dump collect -p 1
# Analyze heap allocations
dotnet-dump analyze my_dump.dmp
> dumpheap -stat

dotnet-counters.description: Live CLI monitor for GC rates, heap sizes, and CPU overhead.

gcroot command.description: Finds the chain of references holding an object on the heap, preventing collection.

gc settings.description: Advanced runtime configuration parameters to tune memory limits and latency strategies in containers.
gc settings.tip: Set memory hard limits to match container limits, preventing OS-level OOM kills.

gc latency modes.description: Modes like LowLatency or SustainedLowLatency to delay Gen 2 collects.

heap hard limit.description: Ensures .NET runtime respects container RAM limits.

strangler fig migration.description: Gradually replaces monolith endpoints with modern .NET APIs. Relies on reverse proxies to mask the backend layout from users.
strangler fig migration.tip: Never do a big-bang rewrite. Slice the domain vertically and migrate module by module.

reverse proxy & yarp.description: Yet Another Reverse Proxy (YARP) is a highly customizable proxy built in .NET. Routes client requests to either old or new apps.
reverse proxy & yarp.tip: Enables seamless URL path takeover by modifying route configurations without client awareness.

path-based routing.description: Routes '/api/legacy' to MVC 4.8, while '/api/newFeature' goes to .NET 9.

dynamic rewriting.description: Modifies headers and cookies on the fly to match target formats.

auth sharing.description: Sharing security sessions between ASP.NET forms authentication and modern JWT/Cookie tokens using Shared Data Protection Keys.
auth sharing.tip: Configure modern ASP.NET Core to encrypt and decrypt cookies matching legacy forms auth schemas.

data protection api.description: Configures shared cryptographic keys in Redis or Azure Blob Storage.

dual authenticator.description: New API validates legacy cookie formats by mapping user claims.

database separation.description: Avoiding shared database lock contention by shifting from a shared database model to modular schema boundaries.
database separation.tip: Use temporary views or ETL synchronizers during migration, but enforce API calls for cross-domain data.

shared db schema.description: Monolith and .NET 9 share tables. Keep transactions short to avoid deadlocks.

logical extraction.description: Introduce separate schemas. Clean code references before splitting database files.

perf types.description: Types designed to bypass heap allocation, reduce copying of bytes, and achieve maximum API throughput.
perf types.tip: Use these in hot paths (JSON parsers, custom middleware, serialization, byte reading).

span<t> & readonlyspan<t>.description: Contiguous block of arbitrary memory. Stack-allocated ref struct representing arrays, stack memory, or unmanaged pointers.
span<t> & readonlyspan<t>.tip: Cannot escape the stack. Cannot be used as class fields or across async await boundaries.

ref struct.description: Forced stack allocation. Prevents object heap promotion.

slicing.description: Sub-referencing arrays or strings in constant time O(1) without allocations.

memory<t>.description: Heap-allocatable representation of contiguous memory. Can be used inside classes, tasks, and across await statements.
memory<t>.tip: Use Memory<T> when memory slices need to persist across async method states.

async friendly.description: Bypasses ref struct limitations. Storable in heap async state machines.

t.span property.description: Obtains temporary Span<T> wrapper for rapid index operations.

valuetask<t>.description: Struct-based alternative to Task<T>. Eliminates heap allocation when an async method completes synchronously.
valuetask<t>.tip: Do not double-await a ValueTask. Convert to Task using .AsTask() if multi-awaited.

zero allocation.description: Saves a Gen 0 class allocation when tasks complete synchronously.

task conversion.description: Use .AsTask() to integrate with Task.WhenAll or Task.WhenAny.

native aot.description: Ahead-of-Time compilation compiling C# into CPU machine code, removing the need for a JIT compiler.
native aot.tip: Requires zero reflection in serialized objects; rely on source generators.

instant startup.description: Bypasses JIT compilation phase. Ideal for Serverless and microservices.

trimming engine.description: Tree-shakes unused assembly code, reducing size and memory footprint.

cqrs & es.description: Decouples data write paths from read queries, representing system state as an append-only stream of historical events.
cqrs & es.tip: Ensure Event handlers are idempotent to handle message duplication.

cqrs segregation.description: Splits mutations (Commands) from read operations (Queries). Different database technologies can optimize both paths.
cqrs segregation.tip: Implement Commands via MediatR + EF Core, and Queries via raw Dapper read replicas.

command model.description: Highly consistent write path. Enforces business rules and validation.

query model.description: Denormalized read path optimized for fast API responses.

event sourcing.description: Saves application state changes as an immutable sequence of events. State is rebuilt by reading and replaying events.
event sourcing.tip: Allows historic system audits and auditing at any point in time.

append-only store.description: Event stream is immutable. No UPDATE or DELETE allowed.

state reconstitution.description: Reads the stream for an Aggregate ID and calls Apply() sequentially.

snapshots.description: Saves state of the aggregate at version N. Speeds up loading by bypassing the replay of thousands of old events.
snapshots.tip: Create snapshots every 50 or 100 events to maintain optimal load speeds.

version storing.description: Snapshots record the exact event version they represent.

replay shortener.description: Loads snapshot, then replays only events occurring after that version.

multi-tenancy.description: Serving multiple customer accounts (tenants) from a single deployment while ensuring complete data isolation.
multi-tenancy.tip: Physical database separation provides the highest isolation and simplest data migration.

isolation levels.description: Three major models: Logical separation (shared DB, shared schema), Schema separation, and Database separation.
isolation levels.tip: Logical separation is cheapest; Separate Database separation is safest and complies with privacy rules.

shared db (logical filter).description: TenantId column on tables. Highly prone to cross-tenant leak bugs.

separate db (physical).description: Each tenant has its own connection string and database files.

tenant provider.description: A scoped service resolving the current Tenant ID from HTTP Request context (subdomains, headers, or JWT claims).
tenant provider.tip: Implement early in the request pipeline using custom ASP.NET Core Middleware.

middleware resolution.description: Inspects host names (tenant1.app.com) or custom HTTP headers.

jwt claim mapping.description: Extracts validated tenant identifiers from the caller's auth token.

ef core query filters.description: Applies filters globally to ensure queries automatically append TenantId checks behind the scenes.
ef core query filters.tip: Use IgnoreQueryFilters() only for admin tools requiring cross-tenant operations.

global query filters.description: EF Core automatically appends 'AND TenantId = @TenantId' to SQL.

dynamic dbcontext.description: Retrieves connection strings dynamically in OnConfiguring based on current Tenant.

async & concurrency.description: Managing asynchronous workflows and multiple threads of execution. Prevents UI freezes and boosts web server scalability.
async & concurrency.tip: Always pass CancellationTokens to async actions to allow graceful task termination.

task parallel library.description: High-level abstractions built on top of the thread pool. Manages Task execution, completion states, and worker threads.
task parallel library.tip: Use Task.Run for CPU-bound tasks, but never for I/O-bound tasks to prevent thread pool exhaustion.

task.whenall.description: Awaits parallel execution of multiple tasks. Fails fast if any task throws.

thread pool.description: Manages queueing tasks and scheduling them across CPU threads.

synchronization.description: Protects critical code sections from race conditions. Limits concurrent access to shared variables.
synchronization.tip: Do not use locks around await statements; use SemaphoreSlim instead.

semaphoreslim.description: Lightweight, async-compatible lock allowing specified max concurrency.

interlocked operations.description: Atomic thread-safe variable updates (Add, Increment) without locking.

best practices.description: Rules to write clean, deadlock-free asynchronous code in modern .NET apps.
best practices.tip: Avoid calling .Result or .Wait() on Tasks. This causes thread pool blocks and deadlocks.
best practices.code:
// ConfigureAwait(false) prevents deadlock in legacy synchronization contexts
await FetchDataAsync().ConfigureAwait(false);

avoid async void.description: Exceptions in async void cannot be caught by callers and crash the process.

configure async context.description: Discard UI synchronization context using ConfigureAwait(false) in libraries.

ef core tuning.description: Understanding EF Core translation and execution steps to optimize database performance.
ef core tuning.tip: Always monitor generated SQL using Entity Framework logging or SQL Profiler.

query optimization.description: Tuning LINQ translations to reduce data sizes fetched over the network and minimize database work.
query optimization.tip: Use AsNoTracking for read-only APIs to bypass state tracking overhead.

asnotracking.description: Bypasses tracking memory caches, yielding faster queries.

projection (select).description: Queries only the columns defined in the select mapping.

loading strategies.description: Configuring how related entities are loaded: Eager, Explicit, or Lazy loading.
loading strategies.tip: Prefer Eager Loading with projections or split queries to avoid N+1 query execution issues.

cartesian explosion.description: Occurs when loading multiple collections in a single SQL query.

dbcontext pooling.description: Reuses DbContext instances from a pool to avoid allocation overhead.

web api perf.description: Optimizing HTTP endpoint response times, CPU cycles, and payload sizing in ASP.NET Core.
web api perf.tip: Implement Output Caching to cache entire HTTP responses on the server.

caching & compression.description: Reducing server workload and download times by caching data and compressing HTTP payloads.
caching & compression.tip: Use Brotli compression for smaller payload sizes compared to traditional Gzip.

response caching.description: Client-side and proxy caching headers configured by the server.

brotli compression.description: Highly efficient compression middleware for text-based payloads.

pipeline middleware.description: Customizing request processing steps using the ASP.NET Core middleware pipeline.
pipeline middleware.tip: Keep middleware lightweight; do not run block-level I/O operations inside request paths.

request lifecycle.description: Sequential execution path of registered middleware components.

global error handling.description: Intercepts unhandled exceptions to return structured Problem Details.

design patterns.description: Applying decoupled design principles and pattern frameworks to write extensible, testable C# code.
design patterns.tip: Do not inject Scoped services into Singleton services; this causes memory leaks and concurrency bugs.

di lifetimes.description: Understanding service lifetimes: Transient (always new), Scoped (once per request), and Singleton (one instance).
di lifetimes.tip: Singleton is ideal for stateless utilities or caches; Scoped is perfect for database contexts.

captive dependencies.description: When a longer-lived service references a shorter-lived service.

scope validation.description: Enabled by default in development to catch invalid lifecycle bindings.

patterns.description: Standard design patterns to write cleaner code.
patterns.tip: Use the Decorator pattern to add cross-cutting concerns (logging, caching) without modifying core classes.

repository & uow.description: Decouples domain logic from database access mechanisms.

options pattern.description: Type-safe bind of configuration sections using IOptionsSnapshot.

security & auth.description: Enforces authentication (identity validation) and authorization (access control) across APIs.
security & auth.tip: Always sign JWT tokens with strong keys and validate expiration dates.

jwt authentication.description: Token-based, stateless authorization. Clients send tokens in the Authorization header.
jwt authentication.tip: Do not store sensitive credentials in JWT payloads; keep claim payloads lightweight.

claims mapping.description: User permissions and identifiers stored inside the JWT token.

refresh tokens.description: Secure, short-lived API access tokens paired with long-lived refresh tokens.

authorization policies.description: Fine-grained access control using dynamic requirement evaluations in ASP.NET Core.
authorization policies.tip: Use policy-based authorization rather than role-based checks for easier maintainability.

policy-based auth.description: Combines claims, roles, and logical checks to grant access.

custom handler.description: Executes C# code dynamically to validate request access.

testing & qa.description: Validating functional correctness, verifying integration pipelines, and measuring API speeds.
testing & qa.tip: Write unit tests targeting business logic, and integration tests for database/API paths.

unit testing.description: Testing single methods or components in isolation by mocking external dependencies.
unit testing.tip: Use NSubstitute or Moq to create test doubles for interface dependencies.

mocking interfaces.description: Simulating external responses to verify code paths.

xunit framework.description: Extensible C# test runner supporting parallel execution.

integration testing.description: Verifies the application runs correctly as a whole, database, configuration, and middleware included.
integration testing.tip: Use WebApplicationFactory to spin up an in-memory test instance of your ASP.NET Core API.

in-memory test host.description: Starts a test server in memory without network port mapping.

testcontainers.description: Launches real database dependencies inside transient Docker containers.

c# memory basics.description: How .NET allocates, handles, and clean up variables during execution.
c# memory basics.tip: Understanding where variables are stored is key to writing memory-efficient C# code.

value vs reference.description: Value types (structs, primitive types) store values directly. Reference types (classes, strings) store a pointer to their heap memory address.
value vs reference.tip: Value types are copied by value; Reference types copy the address reference.
value vs reference.code:
// Value type vs Reference type
int a = 10; // Value type on Stack
User user = new User(); // Reference type on Heap
User reference = user; // Copies pointer address

value types.description: Allocated on the stack. Auto-disposed when scope completes.

reference types.description: Allocated on the heap. Cleaned up by the Garbage Collector.

string performance.description: Strings are immutable. Modifying a string allocates a new string object on the heap.
string performance.tip: Use StringBuilder for string operations inside loops to avoid heap allocations.

string interning.description: Optimization where unique strings are stored in a single table.

boxing & unboxing.description: Boxing converts a value type to a reference type, allocating a heap wrapper. Unboxing extracts it.
boxing & unboxing.tip: Use Generics to avoid boxing when working with lists and collections.

generics benefit.description: List<T> works directly with value types, avoiding boxing.

oop & solid.description: Principles and structures to write clean, reusable, and maintainable C# applications.
oop & solid.tip: Write code that is easy to extend but hard to break.

oop pillars.description: Four core concepts: Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism.
oop pillars.tip: Use access modifiers (private, protected) to enforce encapsulation.

encapsulation.description: Hides object internal state and exposes behavior.

polymorphism.description: Allows derived classes to override base class methods.

single responsibility.description: A class should have only one reason to change.

interface segregation.description: Keep interfaces small; do not force clients to implement unused methods.

linq & collections.description: Allows querying and manipulating in-memory or database data using standard C# methods.
linq & collections.tip: Avoid calling ToList() early in queries to prevent executing queries before filtering.

deferred execution.description: LINQ queries are not evaluated until the query results are enumerated (e.g. in a foreach loop).
deferred execution.tip: Use Where and Select filters before calling ToList() to execute them on the database, not in memory.

ienumerable.description: Ideal for in-memory queries. Filters data on the client side.

iqueryable.description: Ideal for databases. Translates LINQ queries directly into SQL.

collection types.description: Choosing the correct collection type based on search and insertion performance.
collection types.tip: Use Dictionary for fast O(1) lookups by key.

list<t>.description: Contiguous array collection. Fast index lookups but slow insertions.

hashset<t>.description: Unordered collection of unique items. Fast searches.

exceptions & debug.description: Managing errors gracefully in application runtime and identifying issues during development.
exceptions & debug.tip: Use 'throw;' instead of 'throw ex;' to preserve stack trace details.

exception handling.description: Handling unexpected errors using try-catch blocks. Prevents application crashes.
exception handling.tip: Catch specific exceptions first (e.g., FileNotFoundException) before catching general Exception.

finally block.description: Code block that always executes, ideal for closing files and DB connections.

custom exceptions.description: Classes inheriting from Exception to represent specific domain errors.

debugging tools.description: Inspecting application states and executing commands at breakpoints.
debugging tools.tip: Use the Immediate Window in Visual Studio to evaluate variables and run code while paused.

breakpoints.description: Pauses execution at a specific line to inspect variables.

watch windows.description: Monitors the value of variables and expressions dynamically.

rest api basics.description: Exposing Web API endpoints using standard HTTP principles.
rest api basics.tip: Return correct HTTP status codes to help clients handle responses properly.

http protocol.description: Using standard HTTP methods to represent Create, Read, Update, and Delete (CRUD) actions.
http protocol.tip: Use GET for safe read queries; POST for creation; PUT for updates; DELETE for removals.

query vs route param.description: Route params (/users/5) identify resources; Query params (/users?active=true) filter lists.

http status codes.description: 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized.

json handling.description: Serializing C# objects to JSON payloads and deserializing JSON payloads back to C# classes.
json handling.tip: Use System.Text.Json (built-in) for faster performance compared to NewtonSoft.Json.

model binding.description: Mapping incoming HTTP request payloads directly to C# parameters.

camelcase naming.description: Standard naming convention for JSON APIs in ASP.NET Core.

git basics.description: Managing code changes and collaborating with other developers.
git basics.tip: Write clear, descriptive commit messages to keep a clean project history.

commit flow.description: Staging and committing local code changes.
commit flow.tip: Use git diff to verify changes before staging and committing.
commit flow.code:
# Standard git commit sequence
git add .
git commit -m "feat: add interactive mindmaps tab"
git push origin main

staging area.description: Prepares specific file modifications for the next commit.

local vs remote repository.description: Commits are saved locally until pushed to the remote repository (e.g. GitHub).

branching & merging.description: Creating isolated environments to build features and merging them back into the main branch.
branching & merging.tip: Create feature branches instead of committing directly to the main branch.

merge conflict.description: Occurs when two commits modify the same lines of code. Resolved manually.

pull requests.description: Requesting team approval before merging code into production.
